dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Medical Unit 2 for a Stroke/CVA at a home in the city of Richmond.
Hello ma'am, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Left-sided weakness and numbness started this afternoon. I couldn't lift my left leg or arm.
Can you tell me your name and the date today?
My name is Sarah. The date is Saturday the 21st.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-A&Ox4.
I'm going to ask you to squeeze my finger now-can you do that with your right hand?
Yes, I can, but not with the left.
Are you having any double vision or vision issues?
No-I see fine.
You're not slurring your speech and there's no facial droop. How does your airway feel now?
Respirations are 16 and unlabored. I don't see abnormal breathing patterns.
Blood pressure is 119/80 and pulse is 108. She's not pale or diaphoretic.
When did you first notice the weakness, and what happened before that?
I got up this morning at 1000 and had a headache, dizziness, nausea, and vomiting. The left-sided weakness started before I called.
How would you describe the numbness in your left arm and leg?
It feels like sticks in my joints-nothing normal.
I've had a history of strokes-my left side has always been a little weaker. It just got worse today.
On a scale of 0-10, with 10 being the worst pain, how would you describe this numbness and weakness?
Maybe a 4-strong, but not the worst.
Can you tell me what day of the week it is?
It's Saturday the 21st.
Is it different from how it normally feels now, or now more intense?
Definitely worse-just like my last stroke but more severe in the left leg.
Are your pupils reacting to the light? Can you follow my finger with your eyes?
Yes.
Pupils are 3mm, equal, and reactive. No nystagmus.
I'm going to touch your left arm and ask if you feel a sensation. Can you feel me on your right arm now?
Yes on the right, but I can barely feel it on the left.
Your left hand appears slightly contractured. Is that consistent with your baseline or different?
It is slightly different-more contractured.
Pulse is 106 now.
Still 16.
Blood pressure is 127/72.
4-lead ECG shows sinus rhythm-initial observation was sinus tachycardia.
Oxygen saturation is 99 percent.
Initial glucose is 0. I will check again later.
Oxygen saturation was 100 on first assessment.
Placing a 4 lead ECG for monitoring.
Initiating IV access. Using 20-gauge in right forearm. Site has good flashback.
Per protocol 3-5 for stroke, we're following the medical stroke/TIA guidelines.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15-fully alert and oriented. Do you have any facial droop or slurred speech?
No, my face feels normal and I can understand and speak clearly.
Your right leg had no weakness and you could display full range of motion. Your left leg shows signs of acute deficit.
Right arm shows normal sensation and strength. Left arm has decreased sensation and is weak.
Pulse is still 106. Breathing is unchanged.
BP updated to 127/72 with SpO₂ of 99.
EKG still shows sinus rhythm-initial observation was sinus tachycardia.
Glucose is now 97-upward trend but still in the low range.
We'll be taking you to Johnston Willis Hospital-the best facility for stroke patients.
Moving you from the bed now. Please hand us any belongings you need to bring with.
Sarah, let me know if you feel any pain or discomfort as we move you to the stretcher.
I'm okay. Just don't move quickly.
Secured with straps and rails. You're comfortable now?
Yes, thanks.
We'll be leaving shortly. If your condition changes, let us know immediately.
We couldn't collect all personal information, but your medical records-MRN included-are with us.
We've arrived at Johnston Willis Hospital. Transferring patient to CT for further evaluation now.
Vital signs are stable, and patient remains alert and oriented with no signs of facial droop or slurred speech. IV in place, and she's showing signs of left-sided neurological deficits.
Our field impression is stroke-infarction. She's within the treatment window-please expedite evaluation and CT imaging.
She otherwise shows signs consistent with CVA and has no new neurological confusion.
I will ask you again to lift each leg about an inch. Can you do that now?
I can with my right, but my left is still weaker.
Left hand contracture is more pronounced than normal. Right hand has full strength and sensation.
Pulse is still unremarkable and stable.
IV site remains intact. No complications noted so far.
We are moving you up the steps now. Please let us know if you feel any pain or dizziness along the way.
No discomfort at the moment. Go ahead.
Left leg can only lift about 2 inches. Right leg is strong-no issues.
Oxygen saturation is now at 99. No signs of respiratory distress noted.
Blood pressure is stable, and heart rate is still 106-within normal sinus rhythm.
When standing with assistance, your left leg appeared to partially buckle. Is this new or part of your baseline?
New-this is more severe than what I normally experience.
No visible deformity, angulation, or wounds to the left extremities.
Peripheral pulses remain intact on the right. On the left, no significant pulse deficits noted.
Pupils remain equal and reactive to light-no changes since initial assessment.
No signs of nystagmus or visual difficulties observed.
We are now on the stretcher. All straps and stabilization devices are in place.
Please don't hesitate to speak up if anything changes. Your condition is stable for transport, but we're monitoring closely.
Glucose trend shows a slow improvement-still in the low range.
EKG pattern is still sinus rhythm. No arrhythmias observed.
We'll be leaving the residence now. Let us know immediately if you feel any dizziness or shortness of breath.
Good luck during transport. We're ready if you need anything else.
The patient remains alert and coherent. No signs of confusion or disorientation.
No clinical decline noted during the transport phase.
All available devices, medications, and medical records were taken into care.
Patient may experience worsening symptoms during transport; we've repeated all key neurological findings in our notes.
IV remains patent and in good place. No complications to report.
We are right in front of the emergency entrance now.
We have a total of two minutes in transport time. All assessments remain stable since departure.
Final check shows no acute changes.
Pulse still 106, respiratory rate 16 with SPO2 of 99 percent.
Blood pressure is 127/72.
Glucose has risen to 97 from 0. Still in normal range.
Pupil size remains consistent throughout the transport-3mm, equal, and reactive.
EKG machine still reports sinus rhythm-no behavior change.
Patient remained alert and oriented throughout the transport, consistent with A&Ox4 status with no sign of slurred speech.
We have a clear field impression of stroke (CVA) infarction and are following appropriate protocols.
Patient shows signs of an acute neurological event-likely within the treatment window for intervention.
We are confident in the diagnosis and reiterate-no signs of facial droop or slurred speech.
She has a known history of stroke (CVA) with left-sided deficits and recent progression.
She was stable during transport, and all baseline findings were documented.
All available devices, medications, and medical records were taken into care.
Thank you for receiving our patient. We await your prompts for any additional measurements or confirmation.
Patient is prime for CT now and remains stable.
She wished us well and said she's ready for the next step.
She had no discomfort in the stair chair and was easily transferred to the stretcher.
Her speech and mental status were unchanged during the entire call.
All final readings taken and rate no changes.
All transport protocols followed and subjective baseline documented.
No airborne precautions or partial restraints required.
No prior CT or scan imaging events on file.
She was not injured during handling, and proper support was used.
Vital signs confirmed pre-departure, en route, and upon arrival.
No side effects from IV placement or hydration over time.
Her level of consciousness and motor abilities remained consistent.
IV site remains functional and free of complications.
No signs of nausea, vomiting, or abdominal discomfort during transport.
Her medical history and baseline deficits were referenced and documented in care notes.
